policy_statement: Introduce community-wide organic waste collection and decomposition by 2025.
mechanism_description: Collecting and decomposing organic waste outside of landfills diverts putrescible material from landfill disposal, avoiding methane generation and enabling beneficial nutrient/soil recovery.
secondary_justification: Resource Efficiency: Organic waste is converted into compost/digestate, recovering nutrients and organic matter that can partially displace synthetic fertilizers/soil amendments and close material loops.
primary_causal_pathway: Organic waste diversion → ↓ anaerobic landfill decomposition → ↓ CH₄ emissions → ↓ total GHG forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Separate collection and processing of organic/food/yard waste instead of landfilling. (2) Process: Keeping organics out of oxygen-poor landfill conditions reduces methane formation; composting (aerobic) or controlled treatment stabilizes carbon and produces usable soil amendments. (3) Outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle GHG emissions primarily from avoided landfill methane, with co-benefits from nutrient recovery.
dominant_pathway_test: Diverting organic waste from landfills most strongly reduces GHGs by avoiding anaerobic decomposition that generates methane.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: organics diversion prevents landfill methane generation, yielding direct GHG reductions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while compost use can reduce fertilizer-related emissions and improve soils, the largest and most consistent climate-relevant effect across jurisdictions is avoiding high-GWP CH₄ from landfilled organics.  
3) Inherent secondary chain(s): Resource Efficiency is inherent because the mechanism explicitly includes nutrient/soil recovery via compost/digestate production and beneficial reuse.  
4) Edge cases applied: if “decomposition” were implemented via uncontrolled open dumping or poorly managed composting, emissions could rise; however the canonical mechanism assumes managed diversion from landfill with intended methane avoidance and nutrient recovery.  
5) Label applicability: all policies implementing community-wide organics collection/composting share the same core physical pathway (less landfill CH₄) and the built-in material recovery component.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the diverted organics are treated in a way that still creates significant methane (e.g., poorly managed anaerobic piles) or require long-haul transport/energy-intensive processing, net mitigation could be reduced; some implementations may also qualify for Nature-Based benefits if compost application is explicitly used to increase soil carbon, but that is not guaranteed in every instance.
co_benefits: Improved soil health via compost use; reduced landfill leachate/odor/vermin; potential local air-quality improvements; job creation in waste services
instance_edge_case_notes: “Infrastructure-investment” is inferred because community-wide collection and decomposition typically require service expansion and/or treatment capacity; however, the text does not specify whether this is a mandate, incentive, or investment, so classification is based on the most common implementation pathway.
